rule: Order compound index fields as Equality → Sort → Range to match MongoDB query execution model
when_to_apply: Every query with multiple conditions (filter, sort, or range)
description: A systematic approach to compound index design that orders fields as Equality → Sort → Range to match MongoDB query execution model
key_points: Equality fields first narrow the index range efficiently
Sort fields second allow results to be traversed in pre-sorted order
Range fields last use index bounds to filter without evaluating conditions on documents
Field order in compound indexes is critical for performance

sql_instinct_overridden: Create multiple single-field indexes and let optimizer choose
rule: Properly ordered compound indexes eliminate expensive SORT stages from query execution plans
when_to_apply: Whenever queries combine filtering and sorting operations
description: Properly ordered compound indexes eliminate expensive SORT stages from query execution plans
key_points: SORT stages load documents into memory and are computationally expensive
ESR-ordered indexes can reduce query time by 30-40% by eliminating SORT stages
Non-ESR indexes force MongoDB to apply sorts after filtering, wasting resources
Performance benefit grows with dataset size

sql_instinct_overridden: All index fields should serve the same purpose
rule: Order compound index fields according to how they appear in the query: equality conditions, sort operations, then range conditions
description: Specific patterns for how to order compound index fields based on query structure
key_points: Equality only: single ascending field is sufficient
Equality + Sort: put sort field after equality field, match sort direction (-1 for descending)
Equality + Range: put range field after equality field, ascending or descending depending on query
Equality + Sort + Range: place in E-S-R order, sort direction must match query sort

sql_instinct_overridden: Minimize index count to reduce storage overhead
rule: Multi-field indexes justified when they eliminate SORT stages, even if storage and maintenance costs increase
when_to_apply: When deciding between multiple single-field indexes and compound indexes
description: Understanding when index storage and maintenance costs justify performance gains
key_points: Complex compound indexes cost more storage and maintenance but provide larger performance gains
SORT stage elimination justifies the cost of multi-field indexes
Array field indexes incur higher maintenance costs but essential for array queries
Index design should prioritize eliminating SORT stages over minimizing index count

rule: Use explain() output to detect SORT stages and compare index strategies for performance impact
when_to_apply: After creating any index to verify it eliminates unnecessary operations
description: Using MongoDB explain() to verify index effectiveness and detect missing SORT stages
key_points: SORT stage in explain output indicates suboptimal indexing
Comparing explain output across index strategies shows performance impact
Time reduction when SORT stage is eliminated demonstrates ESR guideline effectiveness

sql_instinct_overridden: Sort should happen after filtering in separate operation
rule: Sort field direction must match query sort direction in index definition
when_to_apply: For queries that filter by one field and sort by another
learned: Sort direction must match query sort direction in index definition

sql_instinct_overridden: Put range conditions in separate index
rule: Range fields after equality enable index bounds for filtering
when_to_apply: For queries that combine equality and range conditions
learned: Range fields after equality enable index bounds for filtering

sql_instinct_overridden: Create separate indexes for filter, sort, and range
rule: Sort must come before range in E-S-R order to maximize efficiency
when_to_apply: For complex queries combining filter, sort, and range in one operation
learned: Sort must come before range in E-S-R order to maximize efficiency; eliminates SORT stage

concept: Misconception correction: field order in indexes
previous_belief: Field order in indexes doesn't matter much
corrected_belief: Field order is critical—wrong order can force expensive SORT stages even with an index
rule: Index field order determines query execution plan efficiency
when_to_apply: When debugging why indexes don't improve query performance

corrected_belief: ESR-ordered indexes can provide 30%+ performance improvements by eliminating SORT stages
rule: Properly ordered indexes eliminate expensive operations, creating compound performance gains
when_to_apply: When evaluating whether index optimization is worth the implementation effort
